transcript.transcript: This is Libbie Merrow interviewing Lucy Hurston for the oral histories project. And how are you today? I am most lovely Libbie. So glad to participate in this with you. I am too. I'm glad you're here. So, would you like to begin with your family background? Sure. I'm one of four children born to Everett Hurston and Rachel Black, my mom and dad, in Brooklyn, New York. Really? Bedford-Stuyvesant, between Fulton Street and Saratoga Avenue, and it was a magical, wonderful, lovely time to be in Brooklyn. Oh good. And then the 60s came. You know, in the late 60s and the riots and this and that and everything got scary and we moved to Connecticut. How did you decide on Connecticut? My father knew somebody that lived in Hartford and said: hey, we're in Brooklyn and we need to get out and can you find us something? And we went to this lovely little idyllic New England, town called Bristol. [Laughs] In the 60s. We lived right across the street. We went to private Catholic School the whole time we were growing up in New York. And so when we moved to Connecticut we wound up living across the street from a Catholic K-8 school. It's like perfect, okay. We were catholic. I had received, you know, all of my sacraments and stuff. Born and raised Catholic and went to the Catholic school in Essigeno. You know, because in Connecticut what we found out is the Catholic schools at that time were ethnically divided. So you had St. Stanislaus a polish Catholic church and school, St. Anne's the French and Saint Anthony's, the Italian and they were like: yeah, hey no. When we were growing up in Brooklyn you had to be Catholic to go to a Catholic School, Yes. Okay? And you were allowed to stay in the school as long as you a) paid your tuition and kept up with all the sacraments, okay? And so I was baptized, had my first holy communion and confession, I mean everything. And then we moved to Connecticut when I was preparing to enter eighth grade and it took quite a struggle for me to get into eighth grade at St. Anthony's but Did they let you in easily? They lost okay and so I had to come in. Oh, were you the only black girl there? Yes. Sitting at the front of the room next to sister. Good place to sit? No, makes everybody hate you. You're sitting next sister facing the class. Okay. Yeah, like that. So it was a little wild. I was mostly oblivious to it Good Because I never experienced that before. I just had a lot of hmm moments, you know, like your guts says hmm that doesn't feel good, but you're not really sure what's going on because you have nothing in your background to compare it to. So it was a great learning experience for what would be the rest of my life, you know. But we were pretty isolated from anything severe at that time, you know. Elementary school and then getting into high school was a whole different thing. But my parents, my mom was a stay-at-home mom and she packed lunches and made dinners. I mean, she was, you know, Susie Homemaker. My dad worked for four decades or so for the Brooklyn Post Office. Okay. As a carrier. And when we moved to Connecticut, he wound up working as a security guard. Oh, he worked part-time in New York also at Carver Federal Savings Bank as their security guard. And then when we moved to Connecticut, he took a security guard job at Bristol Press. He worked the third shift, the overnight guarding the newspaper in Bristol. Had a heart attack one night Oh no. Fell in the pit where the machines run and stayed there all night because he couldn't get out because he had a heart attack and all kinds of broken things and they found him in the morning and he was sent to Yale New Haven Hospital for his very first pacemaker. Okay. But, he survived? He survived and several more pacemakers and strokes and lived on to be 90 years old. Oh good. Wow. Yep. My mom lived to be 80. So I come from a long line of long livers. livers. livers. livers. livers. Yep. You'll have a future ahead of you because you're so young. Young is relative my dear. [Laughs] Yep. So you in your formative years were thrown in with lots of white people? Oh yeah. What about in Brooklyn? Was it segregated? We lived in the Italian section of Brooklyn because my grandmother lived next door, right. Grandma Brenner, Frances Lubrano. So we, you know, my mother would send us on Sunday mornings to go get a loaf of Italian bread and the newspaper and a pack of Kool's 100. You could send your eight-year-old daughter store for cigarettes back then and we'd run to the corner, you know, and either go to Novak's the drug store or go to Macaluso's Italian shop and get bread and newspapers and cigarettes and come home to Mama. And go to church sometime in there? And go to church every chance I could get because it was a big girl thing now. In Brooklyn, we didn't go a lot. We weren't allowed to go places, you know. The children weren't allowed to go places by ourselves. So if we wanted to go to Prospect Park one of my parents had to take us, you know. But coming to Bristol and being literally across the street and two houses down from the school, we'd say: can we go play in the playground and my mother would say yes. Because we go to Rockwell Park, which is at the end of our street, she would say yes. And that we felt just new found freedom. The ability to be out of the house either with each other as siblings or with our friends going someplace without our parents. That was just so liberating and fascinating for us. Was it because the town was safer? Yeah, it was. It was small. It was safe. It was what we would just a little bit older, but it was. There were no sirens. There were no fire trucks. There were no gunshots. It just wasn't craziness, you know. That's great. Good. So oh look, your parents dreams and your dreams for yourself? That's an interesting question. That's a very interesting question. The dreams that my parents or guardians or family had for me now it's inevitable, I think, because of the upbringing that I had that I would be a Sociologists. It all makes sense now. There were three girls and one boy in my family. I have two sisters and a brother and I'm the second born. So it's my sister Valerie, me, my brother Ronnie and my sister Georgette. And I always thought everybody's special except me. Val is the oldest one, Ronnie is the only boy, Georgette is the baby and then there's me. Yes. You know, and the three of them were and still are extremely tight. You know, I get in there sometimes but they were just there amigos. So, my mother because of the generation that she was viewed in thought that any services or a surplus of things that the family had should go to the boy in the family because he's going to get a job, he's going to pay for his family, he's going to have a wife, he's going to bring children. We're just going to be like somebody's vessel, you know. So why waste money on you? Education, this or that. Right [Laughs] My thinking was, I spent a lot of my formative years doing modeling as a child, right, for the Great Star Maker Modeling Agency. Okay, that's very interesting. Things a lot of people don't know about me.I was in the little Miss America pageant in Palisades, New Jersey. Really? Oh yeah. So, I thought, okay, since I had this modeling money that I had earned and we could only afford to send one of us. When we moved to Bristol all of us went to elementary school, private elementary school but then when it came to high school, okay, who are we going to send? The money that we've got to be able, well we're going to send Ronnie. My thing was: hey, yo, I think it should be like based on competitive skill and all of this. I was like on Honor Roll, Dean's List, you know. But, no it didn't matter. You could be a smart woman or it, my brother wasn't, but you could be a smart woman or a dumb guy and the funds are still coming to the guy, okay? So they sent him to a private school then? Yeah, St. Paul's yeah, but not me. It was okay, yeah, I seem to land on my feet like a cat with nine lives. Well You know, I mean, I made it through Bristol Central High School. I had this fantasy of what high school was like and I was going to go to the dances, I was going to be the high school cheerleader, I was going to join all the clubs, I was going to do this and I did nothing. I could get into nothing. I tried out every year for cheerleading and they would look at me: No. It was because you were slightly black? Slightly black. Yeah. Any kind of black. Yeah well you know and it's like W.E.B. DuBois says you wonder about it, is that it? Yeah or is it you're inadequate. Yeah, you know, or is it am I inadequate? Am I just not a good cheerleader or is it oh yeah, that black thing? You know, and it just leaves you, it just works on your psyche because I sit through trials, through all other things and I kinda think I'm better than that. I didn't fall. She got on the team and I didn't. I mean in so many ways you just think is it that? It just wears on your self confidence, on your consciousness, you know, just always wondering. If you don't like me, because of me because there's people I don't like and it has nothing to do with their race. Yeah Right? Or anything else. We just don't click, we just don't jive, you know. You're so good to keep trying. Because it couldn't be me. [Laughs] So let me just keep trying but everything happens for a reason. And I'm a firm believer now that doors closed because there can only be so many doors open at a time. And this door isn't your door. Okay. And every time a door closed, here's me, I'm a half glass full person. There's going to be another chance for me. This wasn't the one. So good. So you probably did better in your studies at school because you didn't have Oh, yeah. And I was, you know, the overachiever I was the one that had to make Daddy happy, you know. I make sure that my report card was good and make Daddy proud of me and, you know, that worked to my advantage. You know going on to high school. Okay. So you see how you go on in life was like all those cheerleaders? Yeah. I wonder what's going on in their heads? Anways, yes. So you know, I managed to, you know, I made it through high school. I immediately went to work because I didn't think that I was College material. Really? I was in no way, prepared for or ready for or even told remotely that you could do it when I was going through my high school years. So, but here's the thing, you go to your guidance counselor and you meet with them and they pick your classes and I was, you know, a follow the rules person. This is what I should take, okay fine, that's what I'll take. I had four years of typing. Oh, well, that's all they thought we could do at that time. I had, exactly. I had home economics. I caused such a stink because I wanted to be in woodworking. I thought that was so cool because my work-study job was working for the teacher that ran shop. Doing his paperwork, his invoices and this and that. And I said, well, it'd be cool to like, I'd see some of the things they make. I want to do that. And no, you're taking home economics with Miss LeBlanc. Miss Le Blanc told me to my face. You're gonna be a great pot scrubber for you life. So let's get you scrubbing pots for class Okay Miss LeBlanc we know where you. And I went home to my mommy. My mommy had an unstable portion to her and she went right up to the school and met with Mr. Van Hoff, who was the principal, and Ms. Le Blanc and wanted to know why Ms. Le Blanc said the only black child in her class home telling her whe was going to be a pot washer. When I was in fact a model for Great Star Marker Agencies. My mother kind of went off on them. But those were just the gender restrictions of the time. Women, females, girls took home economics. We had so many weeks of learning to cook and bake and sew a dress and you know. And I was like well I want to cut wood. So high school wasn't all it. I didn't do any proms. Oh, by the fact that I was like way younger than everybody else too. Okay. So I didn't I didn't do dances, proms because I couldn't find even somebody that would say yes to the Sadie Hawkins dance when the girls get to invite the boys. We did The Big Sleepover that we had in high school which was a lot of fun except I went to sleep at ten o'clock. I found a corner and got my sleeping bag went to sleep, okay. But everybody did your thing, you know. I joined the Girls Club in Bristol, which I loved. I even worked there part-time. I worked in the in the little cafe thing putting those little square bricks of pizza in their little microwave or the toaster oven. So I was active and I was happy. Good. You know, I didn't realize that the world was evolving. I'm so amazed In such a negative way around me. I was still happy. Jumping, skipping down the street. I could walk to the library now by myself. I could go to The Girls Club, you know, so it was just having the freedom. You were right on the cusp weren't you? Yeah. And the thing is it was the idea of being able to do what I wanted to make a decision. I'm going to the library today, you know or I would take my sketchpad and I would sit in Packers Field and I would sketch people walking by, you know, and I just always thought stuff like that was cool. That I could choose to like do that. I didn't realize how important that was until after my divorce. We're jumping like decades ahead but it was that the next time in my life that I felt that freedom that I'm going to the store. I don't have to tell anybody where I'm going. I don't have to say what time I'm getting back, you know, and I can decide to stop somewhere on the way and I am unaccounted for. But it was the first time it put that feeling in my head that I'm being restrained and it'll be little brief periods of time that I can flee off by myself and rejuvenate. So did you get married pretty soon right after high school? I got married to run away from home. Oh. I did. I was just, I was so unhappy. And Mom and I weren't like the mother-daughter duo and so the first person that asked me to marry him and he joined the Navy and was moving to Hawaii. I get to leave Connecticut and go halfway around the world and it was I thought this was it. This is my out. Out of the frying pan and into the fire. [Laughs] So is he older? No, he was a dropout from my high school that a girlfriend of mine who we used to ride to, I didn't have a car we used her car. She introduced me to him. He was white. Which pissed off everybody's parent. Okay, his parents. My mother was okay with it. Oh, was she? This is how you would get a step up Lucy. That was my first life lesson, you know. Tie your wagon too. I was like, really? But it allowed me, it allowed her, I was 16 or 17, it allowed her comfort to sign my permission to wed form and get me out of the house. So. But, tht was afer you graduated? Yep. but I graduated what 16-17 years old. And I was in Hawaii by? I graduated in 74, I was in Hawaii by 1975 and my first son Anthony was born in 1976. I call him my bicentennial baby January 7th, 76: 1/7/76. Which is exactly the same day as Zora Neale Hurston's birthday. She was born on January 7th. And so was my first born named Anthony who birthed a daughter named Zora, who has a Mimi, which is me, named Lucy. Zora's mother was named Lucy. I remember that, yeah. My grandmother, I'm named after my grandmother and my granddaughter is named after my aunt. I mean, it's just so well connected, you know. So, when did you start going to college though? Well, we have to live our first life, okay, and then move into our second and third life. So my first life was: married, Anthony, divorced, home. I moved moved back to Southington living with my dad. Did you have another child with the sailor? Oh no. We lived like almost like a Zora Neale Huston parallel life. Let's get married like three times, okay. So we got married once. Yeah, that didn't work. I got married again, had another son. My second husband was black. Well still is. And so I have a biracial child, some would say and according to the one-drop rule I have two black sons. But Tommy was born ten years, nine years later. Okay. Okay. So it's like get married, have a baby, get a divorce, wait a little bit, get married, have a baby, get a divorce, I'm seeing a pattern here. So the next time I got married I was like I done. And I'm telling you, I think I had to go to those first two marriages to earn the perfect husband that I have. I would not have appreciated him as much if I'd have met him at 17. Like I met my first husband. That's true. Because I had to know what bad was to appreciate good You have diffrent standards. And you are a work in progress. Who you are when you married at 17 is nowhere near the person you are at like 30. Not even at 25, right? And so you learn these life lessons and I used to tell my students: you're like a snowball in those old commercials. You just roll along thorugh life just picking up everything that you rub into, if you don't rub into it, if you don't brush up against it, you don't know anything about it but there are those things that you pick up and they become a part of you and you're that growing snowball. The cumulation of your entire life, you know. And that's what happens. I'm a result of everything I have experienced. So when I met Sydney and when I met Sydney, I was dropping off Tommy, my youngest one, with my neighbor because I had a date with somebody else. I told him when I came back: yeah, I want some of that. He's just this big, huge. tall, nice, wonderful, well-spoken, kind, well mannered me and stood up off from the couch when I walked in the room. I was like woah Rhett Butler lives, okay. It was wonderful. And so we went out on a couple of dates and I was like, I told my neighbors that he's marked, he's a marked man, he's mine. I'm just telling you right now and we've been together 30-something years. We've been married 18, no nineteenth, this is so bad because it's on tape. You're allowed to delete anything you want. Okay. We've been married 20 years. This this year will be 20 years. Well, he seems like the perfect husband for you. Very much what you need. Yeah. He's so stable and so And he's educated and well-read, he's the smartest man, I know. But he's kind and he's thoughtful and he doesn't miss a thing. We'll be in conversation about something and I'll be rattling on. I'm the talky one and he's the quiet one. And he will make note of something and I won't even see it. But two months later, he'll say, oh you mentioned that you wanted this, well you mentioned that you liked this, when you were sad the other day when you ate your last bosc pear so I picked up three more for you. I mean, come on. How sweet. There are times I tell him I don't deserve you. And you don't deserve me. [Laughs] We deserve each other, yes. So I started college divorced, twice, with the kids and I was working in Insurance. My first career, my first life was, I spent, you know, 20-something years working in data processing. I started in the typing pool because of all those years of typing. Yeah, man, I left flames on keyboards. You've seen me here in the office. I'm typing and talking to you and watching CNN, okay. And so it got me a job in Hartford in a typing pool and then they said, you know, I think we could use you more than just in the typing pool. And there's a special project that they put me on and it led to them paying for me to take classes to become a programmer. And I was a COBOL programmer. I learned COBOL all the other job control languages: OS, DOS, VCM, DL 1. I mean, it was fascinating. They sent me to school in New York every couple of months at the IBM center, right? To take COBOL, COBOL 2 and I came back and I was working coding for this big huge project. And I loved it. And then I moved from there to the financial systems. And I was DP management in charge of financial systems. The general ledger income statement, balance sheets, all that stuff. And I loved the job twice a month. That would be the 15th and the last day of the month, which is when we got paid, okay? But, that 10 hour day and six day week and on call, up in the middle of the night and it's like, oh my gosh. I just was like you know what I'm done. All right and my father passed and I figured this is a good breaking point. You know if I'm real frugal like I can go to college for a year, you know, with the money my father left. And take a look, go to college and take some college classes and decide what I'm going to do. Because That's wonderful. How old were you then? I was 31 years old. I started at Manchester Community College. When to take an English class because I had sold a couple of short stories here in there and take an English course. Just fit that in. Yeah. Take a sketchbook and write short stories, okay. And so I started at Manchester. They had scholarship funds they told me for black, African-American, single head of household women of non-traditional age. Really? Good. I was 31 black and divorced. We called it: the Lucy fund. Yeah. Okay. And as long as my grades were good I was sponsored. My first semester, i think it was my second semester, I joined Spanish Foreign Language Honor Society, Alpha Mu Gamma. Phi Theta Kappa, the two your honor society, and I maintained my average and graduated with honors and got shipped off to Central Connecticut State University. And I went to Central on scholarship and went Phi Beta Kappa, Alpha Kappa Delta Sociology Honor Society And I received the presidential scholarship to the Ohio State University. Wow Shipped to Ohio and did my master's degree in qualitativ feminist sociology. Tommy was eight at the time I took him with me. [Laughs] What a wonderful journey to the midwest, Columbus Ohio, the Ohio State, largest land grant University in the country. Yes. And the international headquarters of the new KKK. Oh. Yeah, so that made life interesting and it became a learning experience for my son. When I took him to sign him up for school, Elementary School, to reigster him for school. The football coach almost couldn't get across the gym fast enough to meet my son. He said now we're going to win a game. And I thought to myself you know I'm in grad school at Ohio State studying sociology with a concentration on racial and ethnic issues and you're running at me because you've got your first black football player. Let's just lie this stereotype. So we had good and bad times in Ohio. The football coach and I duked it out. He kept saying this: you act like this because you're from the east coast. And I would say my son has to go back to the east coast when I'm done with grad school, okay? So he has to keep up East Coast rules and East Coast grades. Because in Connecticut, if you want to play sports, there's a C rule. You have to maintain a c average, a C in all your classes. Well you're not eligible and I said we're playing by those rules. The Ohio rules are you can't get, I love this, the Ohio academic eligibility rules for sports and extracurricular activity is you cannot get more than two Fs on your report card each quarter. But I was like okay that's just not working enough. Yeah, okay. And so he got a C minus in math, he's not playing bal. That man showed up at my house and I was like, that's nice, I don't recall my father used to say. Was he big or something? Sorry. Oh yeah. He's big. And he's a football player and it's the Midwest, okay. And so they just associate big black boys with being able to play football. Like, they think that my six-foot-six Jamaican husband played basketball. No, he played Cricket, okay? But see this is what all this churning all my life has made me into a sociologist because of this thinking. Why do you think he's good at football? Just because you've got a black student on your football team now? And he's a student: not an athlete. I said you want him to play for you? He needs to get that grade up. They got a tutor. [Laughs] They wanted him bad but it's like those are the rules. Did he play? Yep. Not the first year. He worked on tutoring and after school we sent him to everywhere to get him tutoring. And he was able to do it and guess what? He didn't go to the NFL, okay? So those things used to But he did get into college though? Yeah, you know. Well I started teaching at the community colleges and I started at Naugatuck Valley Community College because Manchester Community College where I went had a hiring freeze. So, I said, I told them, I'll be back, I'm going to Naugatuck because they got an opening right now, okay? I'll get some experience there and sure enough three years later I leave there I go to Manchester. I spent my entire teaching career at community colleges because I believe In what community colleges do and the mission and vision that they have and the population that they serve, okay? And I was so moving when your friend came to the chuch. Oh, church. Jahana Hayes? Jahana Hayes. Okay, who's now a representative in government and I tell you what brought me to tears that day when I brought her for Women's History Month, to talk to the church, when she. I had no idea what she was going to say, right? And she was my student like 15 years ago and one of the first words out of her mouth and brought me to tears, I don't know if you remember, when I was standing in front there with her, she said just remember that there are always different ways of being and doing and oh my God, I had shivers, my knees buckled. Those words came out of my mouth as part of my lecture and teaching about culture. Oh. There is more than one way to be and do. And sometimes if we learn other ways that we don't know about, we could trade cultural things back and forth between populations to the betterment of everybody. But there's more than, I would say to them if you want to study that get out of my class. Go down the hall to mathematics. Mathematics has one right answer: five and five is ten all the time. Sociology, there's a lot of answers to that one question and with most of you guys, most of them are right. Good. Right? So, I mean to hear that, not only, I tried, but I can't think that I contributed something to Johana Hayes formation, but that my words, she remembered came out of her mouth and it's how she works her district and how she works in Washington. You know. That's beautiful. I've heard that from lots of the other people. It finally dawned on me. Oh yes, you don't see the fruits of your labor, okay. It's got to be enough to know, and I was so corny, it's got to be enough to know that you're going to classroom every day and plant seeds. And my job is not to see how those seeds grow. Its to plant them because they may not even know a) that they got seeds, b) that they got the seeds that they can do something with and when they figure it out they'll pull it together. Yeah And that's what I saw that day in church with Jahana Hayes. You know I like to think that I and sociology and Manchester Community College gave her the formation, the foundation, to go forward with that. My father would say on a solid foundation, you can build anything. And I think Community College is that foundation for so much of our population. You know, the people that SAT scores are going to get them into Duke and Yale, you know, they're going to be fine, okay? But the community college students, it's their second chance, third chance, fourth chance, don't have the money, can't leave home, got to work a job, all of that. And yet, the junior college mission as it was formed, you know, decades ago still is required. It's infuriating when you look at it from the academic standpoint because graduating top of your list from a failing school still isn't going to get you, from a failing high school, still isn't going to get you in a good college because they know an A from that school isn't worth an A student from like Avon or Farm, Loomis Chaffee. Yeah. Okay. Or Miss Porter's. So there's still that stratification and equality thing. That's all I've studied my whole life, you know. So it's been wonderful to know that I spent my academic years in the disenfranchised portion of the higher educational system to help people beat the system. Because, everybody, the job offers, Oh a Hurston, you can go here, you can go there. I could. Yeah I could but I choose to be at Manchester. Why should all the cream of the crop be at the Ivy League schools? You continue to entrench in that structure that measure of inequality that better schools get all the good teachers, all the good professors, you know, all the experienced people, the high-end educators that never get to see a community college class. I brought Henry Louis Gates to the campus of Manchester Community College. Oh really? Okay. So my students say we had an opportunity to hear this person, to see this person. I took three busloads of students to the first Obama inauguration. Oh my goodness. I mean we worked Habitat for Humanity in Hartford, in Manchester, for five years running in New Orleans in the Lower Ninth. So I find myself sopping up everything that I've experienced like that snowball during my life and sharing it with students at the community college level who think that those experiences, that those opportunities only come to them at the University level. I teach them the economic affordability, the wise choice, to pay less for your first two years worth of common education courses for credit then you're going to pay at UConn, you know. You could pay so much less. Look class with 40 students and a teacher that knows your name, okay, to take English 101. Okay? Or math 101, all your general educations. Take the first two years at the Community College, you're going to be in a room with 400, 500, 800 students in an auditorium for English 101, for your Soc 101 at UConn, at UMass, you know? I am a firm believer. That's wonderful. In the community college. It's God's work for sure. Yeah. So we've covered almost everything here. How has religion shaped your life? So, how did we get you away from the Catholic Church? Well, you didn't get me away. I was just a, what do you call it, a free radical [laughs] floating around because the Catholic Church no longer worked for me. Yeah And I was very sad because I had like drank the Kool-Aid. I was all in. I wanted to be a nun and my mother beat my butt. I was so, Sister Rosemary Weaver, I adored her. I wanted to be a nun and my mother said, no, because you're having my grandbabies. Someday. That's right. That would have been, had you. Yeah. So, I started out Catholic and then when I got divorced and I did not initiate the divorce, so I was just like an unwilling participant in a divorce. So when I got my divorce, I continued to go to church, you know, and after a couple months the priest said: you know you can't continue to receive the sacraments. Because I was gone for communion when it was coming. Yeah, but do continue to come to church Lucy and bring your envelope. We appreciate your support, you're one of our most fervent supports, that's like, I'm putting gas in somebody else's car. No. Pay for somebody else to get to heaven? No. So I dropped religion for a while. I was like, how could you do this? It wasn't my divorce and I still wanted to attend church. So I stopped that. I tried a couple of other places when we went to Ohio. I said, okay, let's try a church out here. So the Midwest churches are like mega churches. I mean they are you think our little church here in Bloomfield, no. Okay. These are mutant, monster churches, okay. They're like post-apocalyptic buildings you could live in. And we walk into church one Sunday and we were swarmed by Debbie well-meaning. Debbie well-meaning white people. But they were just so shocked that a black person walked into their church. It was too overwhelming. They descended over the two of us. They didn't like it? No, it was just, we were the novelty. Yeah. And they let us know we were the novelty. I image they were trying to get integrated. You know, and they're just like we got one. [Laughs] And they try too hard and it literally it frightened us, okay. And we were out of there man, and we found I said, let's just ride around and this was it. That's ride about and look at the neighborhoods, okay? And let's see what neighborhoods have church signs up and what churches look like they were involved in the neighborhood. And we found in Ohio. Cincinnati United Methodist Church and they had a soup kitchen. Oh. And I was like, oh my God, they're doing it in the community and it was a poor community, a struggling upcoming community. I mean, it wasn't dangerous or anything, it was just poor, you know. We did fish fries. I mean it was a wonderful active and community involved church. And when we left there and came, when I finished graduate school and I came back to Connecticut, we went to a United Methodist Church that I found to be somewhat divisive. And so I tried and then I gave up. And I was done with church and I don't know, a friend of mine from Town and Country Diner, you know, said you need to come to church with me tomorrow. And I was like, yeah, I don't do church. I said I'm a spiritual person. I believe I have a one-on-one relationship with God, with Jesus, and I asked sister Rosemary, one time, so, if God is everywhere, why do I have to get up out my bed when it's cold and snowy and come across the street to church? Can I just talk to him from there? Unfortunately, I ask that in the middle of religion class and I got whacked for that one. Oh. But I said, okay, I'll try it. And I came here. And this is home. Who was the preacher then? Ed. Pastor Ed Ayala. Oh yeah, he was good. Oh my God, I loved him. You know and we spoke Spanish together because I'm fluent in Spanish. I mean it was just The Best Is Yet To Come. You know you just associate sayings with people like I am not a criminal? [Laughs] The Best Is Yet To Come. Now it may have been, I don't know, if that's a saying for this church or this denomination of churches or if it was a Pastor Ed thing. It was a Pastor Ed thing. But, oh my God, I loved that. It filled me and so when the Methodist Church that I had tried was having real difficulties and resistance to the Community Kitchen, I came to the mission and I was like, you know, I got this idea. And I spoke to pastor about it, I spoke to a couple people and before you know it, this church rises to the occasion and does what it's supposed to do. Which is be in service to a population other than one hour on Sunday. Okay? I mean that's all I want, I wanted a church that's a community. That works things other than service and church and money. And so they took on the community kitchen and it's been an outstanding contribution in so many ways. Food, clothing, shelter, comfort, community. Getting people in touch with services that they need for 211. You know, I mean, just so much that we do. During Covid, the meal deliveries. That's food, clothing, and shelter, basic human rights and any person or organization that works toward that has a spot in my heart. Great. I'm glad. And so food share and Habitat for Humanity: my big two causes. Because you can't be a good Christian if the people around you are homeless, exposed, hungry, doing without, lonely, mentally ill, medically unchecked, all of that. The Human Condition. Unless you are treating all of The Human Condition, you can't call yourself a Christian and I don't care what denomination you are you can't be a Christian if people are suffering around you and you have the means to reduce some measurement of that suffering and you do nothing? You're not a Christian. And I used to tell my students, I have all these great little stories, so imagine you're walking through the woods, it's beautiful, its Penwood State Park and there's cliffs and this and that and the trees and it's Autumn and it's beautiful and you're loving the environment and in the distance you hear: help, help. And you keep walking and you get closer: help. The clouds are getting louder and you get there and you find the person who is hanging by their fingernails off the cliff, going to fall three thousand million miles into that gorge below them. Not your fault. You didn't push them, you didn't trip them, you are not responsible for the position they are in. However, you continue your little wonderful walk and that person falls and dies. That's on you. Because you didn't even try. You may have tried and still failed, but you didn't even try. I miss teaching. [Laughs] So religion is a part of my life in that. Yeah. I believe in it's good. I believe in all the different ways that it tries to reach people to make them good. Yes. You know? And for some of us that's the community kitchen and for some of us, I'm home after a bad car accident, you know, so I'm not physically able to be in the community kitchen with them and they are champions in there but I can do what I can do. I am home knitting little tiny prayer shawls. Are you really? Yeah, you know. It's like, it's what I can do, you know? Well, baking you see me on Facebook and Instagram. Well, I just admire you so much because you got the retirment happened, and the accdient happened Yep. and your life changed completely. And you became the most wonderful sourdough baker in the whole world. And so here is a bag full of sourdough scones for you. Blueberry and raspberry that I made. [Laughs] Oh, thank you so much. I can't tell you. That's wonderful. We'll talk about sourdough after the interview because I do have questions. And also you're a wonderful gardener. Yes, I love to garden. It's making things. It's it's like birth. I made those children. Yes. And there may be some other contributions but I made those children and it's taking my hands and putting it in the dirt and making stuff. I mean, whether it's flowers. I've got a beautiful flower garden. I've got two herb gardens. And we've got three food gardens and we grow, I would say 40 to 50 percent of our food. Good. We have 300 gallon rain barrels out there. Oh yeah I got those last year. Yeah, and we grow. I've got an outdoor freezer that's filled with all of our green beans, our collards, our mustards, our carrots. I mean, we grow everything. All of my spices: thyme, rosemary, I mean everything. Lots of peppers, and we have a salad bar we call it. A raised bed outside that only has salad stuff in it, you know, tomoatoes, regular tomatoes, little tomatoes, lettuces, cucumbers, that's in the salad, radishes, that's in a salad garden. Great. So it's just fun, you know? And then we package it up. We give it away. I bake for my neighbors. I produce probably three or four sourdough breads a week and just give them away, you know. We have two elderly ladies in the neighborhood that I have adopted and they get a small bread, they're by themselves, they get a small bread and they get a pastry. Something baked out of the diabetes cookbook, okay. But it's also a well-check, you know, because it is women in their 90's and they're living in a house by themselves, okay, and they've got no family around them, right? So I'm your family, okay. So it's a well-fair visit. I go and watch Jeopardy with this one, you know. on certain nights and you know but isolation is dangerous. I know. But, you don't have to do that. So many people would just turn their back. But you know what? When I'm 94 somebody, better knock at my door. Do what you want but do for others what you would like done to you. I would not want to be 90 years old, and sitting in a beautiful, lovely house all by myself. I certainly relate to that because I don't have to. So, what you moved to Bloomfield 40 years ago? Yes. From Bristol to get married. I lived over on the other side of town With you Hawaii husband? Oh no, I left him in Hawaii. Now, I really did. I left him in Hawaii. This was husband two. When I moved to Bloomfield and we built this house that I'm in now. Tommy was, we moved in in February and Tommy turned two in March. Tommy's 37 now. So, you and your second husband built that house? Mhm and then he got stupid and then he was gone and I was grateful for that, you know. And then it was that freedom period again. Yes You know. It's like I can go anywhere, I think I'm gonna go to lunch today all by myself. This was you know I'm in my 30s and this was still a major thrill for me that I can go unsupervised, unchecked, you know I could do what I want, you know, and it was just I have been in the care of a man, my father, husband one, husband two, for most of my life. And Sydney my current husband, myy life soulmate, is like, well, go and have a good time. He doesn't ask me where I'm going. I put sticky notes on the bathroom mirror, got to 9:00 yoga class and he says, you don't have to tell me where you're going, you know. Don't you care? Nah, but I'm very fortunate that all of my experiences cumulatively the good and the bad have made me the person that I am. And I am especially. Well, the rest of the world's fortunate for that. Yeah. [Laughs] So, that's when you first moved to Bloomfield? It must have been a brand new neighborhood then? No, we moved to the other side of town and when that was over then this house was built and I've been there ever since. We just finished our addition, so you have to come over. So we've got our gardens and we've got great, because covid, we have great outdoor space. Good You know so that we could just be home, you know. Our screen porch, our gardens, the little pool, the inflatable pool for the kids, and the sprinklers and real fire pit. And we just made, we had projectors out in the backyard watching movies at night with the kids, grandkids, we enjoyed covid. [Laughs] I set up delivery of everything. We were going nowhere, okay? So we established the milkman, okay, and all my milk and dairy products came from them. We ordered all our groceries, We became like Amazon queens and kings. We weren't going anywhere. We just lived. We kept our circle tight because of the grandbabies. You know, I've got one grand baby that had RSV, you know, the respiratory virus. So she's got compromised lungs. So yeah, she's gets exposed to on one during covid except that inner circle. You know, so we kept a tight circle so you know that the grandbabies and everybody stayed safe, That's so nice. You never caught it yet? No my husband and I nope, knock on wood. [Knocks on Wood] What I knock on is all those booster shots. Thank goodness for this church that arranged to set up this place as an inoculation center. Yes. You know, to be working with the Bloomfield West Hartford Board of Health You're probably due for another one? I'm due this month for my 6 month. I had my first three full shots and then a booster and a second booster. And now I'm due this month, the end of this month, I'm due for my third booster. And we were just talking, my husband and I, were just talking about this the other day. He's like you gonna to do it? I was like: I want to live I have grandchildren. I have to live to be like 600. So no harm, no foul. The other shots didn't kill me. Yes, I'm getting my booster. Yeah. You know I've got Graves Disease, I'm immunocompromise, you know, my husband has a condition and he's immunocompromised. So it's like no, we're not taking any chances, you know, we still mask in crowded, so we avoid crowded situations. But you see when we come to church on Sunday we're still masked. Any time were around more than three or four people that we know we're masked. I would not want to be the one to go someplace, catch something, be asymptomatic, and give it to our grandchild. Oh yeah. You know. Uhuh, not on my watch. So I am a scientist, a social scientist, but a scientists nonetheless, and the facts are the facts, you know, and I follow scientific fact above everything else. It's gotten us this many millennia, okay? It's gotten you everything. Your sourdough bread, your garden. I mean it is. I'm reading a wonderful book now called Chemistry Something. It's a novel. Oh, I got to read that. I'm halfway through. I'm devastated that he died. Okay, Mr. Evans died, I haven't read it. You haven't read it? No. Okay, I'm going to read it. That's okay it's in the first 20 pages. I didn't spoil the ending. But yeah I mean chemistry is in everything. One of my daughter-in-laws is a chemist, okay. So when covid happened and she does lab work, so when covid happened I was like, okay, so you are a lifeline here. My oldest son, a paramedic, we've got medical people in the family. I've got an in-laws sibling that's an ICU nurse, okay? So yeah, we got all the medical people, we can call them for everything but we just listened to the CDC, the health department, we kept track of the figures when you know I remember when they said 3% in Bloomfield oh my goodness and then it was like 25%, you know, and 15% now is good. But you just have to try not to hurt anybody in the world. That's it. My girlfriend of mine a very, very dear sweet close girlfriend of mine, her favorite saying and now my favorite saying is we are just here to walk each other home. Oh, I like that. One of my favorite is: be swift to love, make haste to be kind. That's a T-shirt. Oh, my god. Life is short. We have not much time on this Earth to walk with each other. So make haste. So, why did you choose Bloomfield instead of West Hartford or Bristol? This is where the fiance was. The what? This is where the fiance was. Oh, where the fiance was. Who had a house already. So, I was living in, Nothing about it being an intergrated town or anything like that? I knew nothing of Bloomfield. I was living in Bristol and we were dating, we got engaged, he had house in Bloomfield and when we got married that's where we moved. Good. You know. That's the only reason I came to Bloomfield. At the time that I moved to Bloomfield, I do remember a sign, a sash sign hanging, I don't remember where, the most All-American town, the All-American town of Bloomfield. Yes. The All-American town. I remember that. And I said: that's so nice that we're talking about the racial composition of the schools and stuff. This is like a really nice town. It's very mixed, it's very, you know, and it's very New Englandly, you know. With the town green and the activities that they did and the farmers market. And then things started changing and rapidly and at that time, I think I missed a lot of what could have been great examination of situations, but I was going through the divorce. So, I was. How do you mean things changed? How Bloomfield was changing. Oh, becoming more African American? Becoming less white. I was watching white flight. Okay, and I couldn't figure out why? We scorned those people. They were so unlucky. Really? I just think they're just missing so much. I don't understand it. It's a waste of human capital It is. To throw people away for any shallow reason because that person you threw away and if we look at our past, we will see this to be true, and if we look to our past we won't repeat the same mistakes but the guy that died on the hospital steps because he was negro, colored and couldn't get in, is the scientist that developed pulling platelets out of blood. I mean a brilliant person like that but you can't treat him because this hospital doesn't treat negro patients and he died on the steps of the hospital. So if you're willing to throw away, human capital, you deserve what happens to you. I just can't see wasting human potential. Something that somebody could contribute to a society, to a family, to a community, to a medical issue. A To each other. You know, to just throw them away and decrease their value or give them no value. You know, and it sometimes happens because people have to step on others, to squash them down so that they feel like they're big. That's what we learned in college. Yes. Yeah. It's only the insecure that are So, what else you want to talk about? As you can see, I can talk like forever. You're a good talker. I'm a teacher. Oh, let's do on the back page, number 13, what Memories do I have, oh, activities. These are good questions. Nicest town designation, this was just recently by the Reader's Digest. Where is that? Number 11. So I don't anything about that. The Readers Digest, It was on the board in front of Arace, Carmen Arace Elementary School. Did we apply? I know read Reader's Digest like my father did, oh my God, I can't believe it, right. But there's all these contests, right? And let's vote for the nicest town in America and Bloomfield was one of those towns that was named in the Reader's Digest survery. I think it's a nice town. Me too. I love my town. I live on a cul-de-sac. I love my community. We have our own Facebook page. We have In your neighborhood? Oh yeah, man. And we have, we're a very good neighborhood. We're a very tight neighborhood. I put things, my excess from the garden, at the end of the driveway and come and get it, okay? And My herb buckets, like a massive bucket of thyme, of rosemary, of sage, and it's just out there, just come, you do a little thing, just clip it and go. And we used to do, pre-covid stuff, we used to do Halloween walks. So we would take the kids out, right, and there's, I mean it's a cul-de-sac, we take the kids out and we do the cul-de-sac walk with them. And then that evening, we get our mugs out and we do the adult walk, drinky, drinky, at everybody's house, right? At the end of the summer at the top of the cul-de-sac where everybody brings something and we have a big cookout, we play our music, we dance in the street, right at the top. We invite like all of the politicians and anybody wants to stop by, you know. We have a fun neighborhood so much so that my next door neighbor, who is one of my other best friends in the world, my snow guy cuts, does my driveway, does her driveway, and then cuts a path between our driveways so we can run back and forth, okay. I mean it's just, it's wonderful. Like I said, we've got elderly people in the neighborhood that are by themselves and we include them in stuff. We'll go and pick them up, bring them over for the once-a-year cook out in the backyard and you know, make them a part of a family. So has everybody been living there for quite a long time? I think we've had two major neighborhood turnovers, you know. Everybody's kids are grown and you know. I think there's only three of us that are left that are, three or four houses, that are the original homeowners. We built, we're still there. But we watched like all of our kids grow up together, take the bus, we were our own bus stop, take the bus to school together, grow up, go to college, get married, they're back with grandkids now and the house turns over, right? So now, we've got new littles in the neighborhood for the third time, right. Which is just lovely for the grandparenty people that are left. They have these little ones, you know, 3,5,9 out there. And I tell you I'm going to have to do something about the speed on our street because they're tearing down that to the stop sign from the cul-de-sac like, it's Indy 500 and there's so many littles there now. Will speed bumps do anything? That's what I want to put, like six foot high speed bumps. Six foot high? [Laughs] [Laughs] They got to slow down, okay. Nah, I would love to put a speed bump right in the middle of the halfway distance between there, or what they call sleeping policemen, is a speed bump. I would love to see one or two speed bumps be put on our street. Have you asked for them yet? Oh, I will. Good because they were trying to have an initiative to make streets safer. Yeah, I did this survey. Yeah, Of course, I do all the surveys that come to me because I'm a good sociologist. We do surveys. And number 13 was the other one I was looking at. This is when I came to town. When I moved to town and Anthony was little before I had Tommy, we used to go by Capaco and Copaco butcher was still there and it had the animals out in front. We'd go, which is probably really bad, we'd go to McDonald's and get an extra thing of french fries and then go feed the farm animals, okay. We'd feed the goats and the llamas and they had all these animals out in front of Capaco. That was fun, wasn't it? You know, it was so much fun and then you could go to the butcher shop and order what you want for Thanksgiving, for Christmas, for Easter, you know. Park Avenue Pharmacy, with the parakeets, I remember that. AC Peterson. I was president of the Bloomfield Little League, okay. Oh, were you? For years I was in charge of little league and it was a wonderful transition from, you know, all the old white guys that have been running it forever. And then there was some mommies that started getting involved as like assistants. We bring snacks, we'd help keep score, we packed their their gear up for them and stuff and then we started sitting in the dugout with them and then we started saying, you know, we could do this, okay? And I got together two other women and we coached the team the next year. Then we started recruiting girls and that's when they got pissed. And I said, but I read the charter, I read the Bloomfield Little League Charter from Williamsport, Pennsylvania and everybody can play girls and boys, Oh they did not want girls in there. That's when they started backpedaling from the admistration. I was like, I'll take it. And I recruited every girl in town. I went to, all the schools had flyers girls and boys. You know who was on my team over the years? Nykesha Sales. Woah. The absolute best pitcher in the world. She'd go, yo coach, where do you want it? She'd get the bat, where you want it and I think over those trees. Okay. She's stepping the box and delivered She could hit, she could field, she could pitch. We had Ricky Romero, who pitched so fast, I mean the kids were scared. They would like barely get in the box. They were like, so scared and my son, my oldest, my Anthony was a catcher and he broke his thumb off one of Ricky's catches, right? I'm umping, I'm behind him, right? And he goes ow, and I'm like, we've got two more out, I'm tapping him on his helmet, my son, and he's crouched down as the catcher and I'm banging on his helmet. We got two more outs to go. We got to do this before it gets too dark. Let's go. Get back down there. It can't be that bad. He took two more strikes in that hand Oh. And then we're walking back to the car and he said oh my thumb is swelling, oh I'll give you a can of Coke you could hold onto it, it's cold. By the time we got home he had like mutant swollen. I was like okay, let's go to the walk-in and they said yes it was broken. And he said yeah, it got broken on the first pitch, but she made them pitch two more at me. And I was like, Doctor I need to explain before you call DCF, alright. But a lot of good memories in this town, you know, the little league and the years that I had it was totally co-ed. I added t-ball to it. We had a Babe Ruth to it. So it really expand and everything and then once I left they decided that all the girls were going to be put into the girls softball. Oh. So, that one step forward, but two steps back. That's us, right. It is, it's like Roe, no Roe. One step forward, two steps back. So but it's a wonderful little town. It's safe. Its thoughtful. I love the concert on the greens. I love our Christmas parade. In front of the church, the first year they did this, my granddaughters were angels for the living nativity scene. And then somebody brought a baby by and the baby wasn't there long. But I mean it's just so many wonderful things that they do. Yeah. It's been a great time. I think the church has a little bit to do with it too. Oh definitely. Yeah. Gosh, what time is it now? I don't wear a watch anymore since I'm retired. I literally gave up wearing a watch. Why? Just because My life was driven by watch after 12:30 classes. It's 12:30 to 1:50, I've got 10 minutes to go to the bathroom let's water before class. No. Yeah. Everything was dependent on time. I no longer wear a watch. Good. I freed myself. Maybe I should turn this off now so we can talk about sourdough. Okay. Are we? I think we're good. Yeah, it's been a wonderful interview, so glad you Thank you. I haven't heard about you're writing though. You still writing? I'm in the middle of, I just finished a children's book and I'm about two to four weeks away from finishing my memoir. So your prime for this interview? Yeah. I was like, shit this is a rehearsal, you know, its wonderful. You still do your art? Your sketching? You know, there's a lot of things that I do and you don't have to be good at them, you just have to enjoy them, okay? Absolutely. So I sketch, I suck. I paint and you see what my husband does with painting, so I don't let him see any of my paintings. Because he's a genius. He's an award-winning artist. I didn't know that. He's won several years at the CRT Art competition: best in show, first place, I mean he's just phenomenal. He's at the Mystic Art Show, Art Exhibits. He shows down there. Pastor Shawn keeps saying he needs to have an exhibit here at the church. And I hope that the last exhibit that he saw will prompt him to do so. When you come by the house, every single piece of artwork hanging in our house, is done by him. We mad him pinky swear that we would hang nothing. that wasn't one of his original works. Yeah. That's great. Well, we should at least have something down here. You do Where? That is it that one? Oops, I can't go I'm on a microphone. When we finished, that, that's not it. When we do our sourdough talk, I think it's outside its on that wall. There it is. Yes, it's right. outside the office on the wall. That? Yeah. Right on the left. Gosh that's amazing. No, right on the left. Okay, I'm going to turn this off. Goodbye folks. Bye, this has been fun.

lines.line: # Introduction and Family Background

> Libbie Merrow interviews Lucy Hurston as part of the oral histories project. Lucy begins by describing her family background, being one of four children born to Everett Hurston and Rachel Black in Brooklyn, New York, and recounts her experiences growing up in Brooklyn until her family moved to Connecticut in the late 60s.
** introduction, family background, Brooklyn, New York, interview, oral histories project **
keywords: ** introduction, family background, Brooklyn, New York, interview, oral histories project **

metadata.title: Introduction and Family Background
metadata.synopsis: Libbie Merrow interviews Lucy Hurston as part of the oral histories project. Lucy begins by describing her family background, being one of four children born to Everett Hurston and Rachel Black in Brooklyn, New York, and recounts her experiences growing up in Brooklyn until her family moved to Connecticut in the late 60s.
metadata.keywords: introduction, family background, Brooklyn, New York, interview, oral histories project

lines.line: # Relocation to Connecticut

> Lucy discusses the family's decision to move to Connecticut due to the unrest in Brooklyn in the late 60s. They settled in Bristol, Connecticut, where Lucy continued her education at a Catholic school, amid experiences of ethnic divisions in the schooling system.
** relocation, Connecticut, Bristol, education, Catholic school, ethnic divisions **
keywords: ** relocation, Connecticut, Bristol, education, Catholic school, ethnic divisions **

metadata.title: Relocation to Connecticut
metadata.synopsis: Lucy discusses the family's decision to move to Connecticut due to the unrest in Brooklyn in the late 60s. They settled in Bristol, Connecticut, where Lucy continued her education at a Catholic school, amid experiences of ethnic divisions in the schooling system.
metadata.keywords: relocation, Connecticut, Bristol, education, Catholic school, ethnic divisions

lines.line: # Childhood Experiences

> Lucy reflects on her experiences growing up in a predominantly white environment after moving to Connecticut. Despite racial isolation, she shares memorable moments of newfound freedom compared to her restricted childhood in Brooklyn.
** childhood, ethnic isolation, freedom, Connecticut, racial challenges **
keywords: ** childhood, ethnic isolation, freedom, Connecticut, racial challenges **

metadata.title: Childhood Experiences
metadata.synopsis: Lucy reflects on her experiences growing up in a predominantly white environment after moving to Connecticut. Despite racial isolation, she shares memorable moments of newfound freedom compared to her restricted childhood in Brooklyn.
metadata.keywords: childhood, ethnic isolation, freedom, Connecticut, racial challenges

lines.line: # Education Struggles and Achievements

> Discusses the gender and racial challenges Lucy faced in education, touching upon support for her male sibling over her, despite her academic achievements. She shares her experience trying to join extracurricular activities like cheerleading amid racial prejudice.
** education, gender roles, racial prejudice, extracurricular activities, struggles **
keywords: ** education, gender roles, racial prejudice, extracurricular activities, struggles **

lines.line: # First Marriage and Family Life

> Lucy shares her experience of her first marriage that was partly an escape from family life. She reflects on the cultural and familial context of her decision and life in Hawaii, which led to her first son's birth.
** marriage, family life, Hawaii, cultural context, escape, first son **
keywords: ** marriage, family life, Hawaii, cultural context, escape, first son **

metadata.keywords: marriage, family life, Hawaii, cultural context, escape, first son

lines.line: # Return and Education Resurgence

> After her first divorce, Lucy returns home and eventually goes back to school at Manchester Community College, supported by scholarships. Her academic journey leads her to a master's degree in sociology.
** divorce, education, Manchester Community College, sociology, scholarships **
keywords: ** divorce, education, Manchester Community College, sociology, scholarships **

lines.line: # Teaching Career and Community Impact

> Lucy delves into her teaching career at community colleges, emphasizing their role in providing second chances. She recounts her community involvement and contribution to social equality through education.
** teaching, community colleges, education, social equality, impact **
keywords: ** teaching, community colleges, education, social equality, impact **

lines.line: # Religious Journey

> Lucy shares her journey with religion, from her Catholic upbringing to her eventual distancing from organized religion after feeling disillusioned. She discusses finding her spiritual community in the United Methodist Church.
** religion, Catholic, United Methodist Church, spirituality, community **
keywords: ** religion, Catholic, United Methodist Church, spirituality, community **

lines.line: # Community Involvement in Retirement

> Expressions of gratitude and community involvement are highlighted as Lucy discusses her engagement in gardening, baking, and supporting her neighborhood post-retirement while managing health challenges.
** retirement, community involvement, gardening, baking, health challenges **

children.text: # Introduction and Family Background

> Libbie Merrow interviews Lucy Hurston as part of the oral histories project. Lucy begins by describing her family background, being one of four children born to Everett Hurston and Rachel Black in Brooklyn, New York, and recounts her experiences growing up in Brooklyn until her family moved to Connecticut in the late 60s.
** introduction, family background, Brooklyn, New York, interview, oral histories project **
# Relocation to Connecticut

> Lucy discusses the family's decision to move to Connecticut due to the unrest in Brooklyn in the late 60s. They settled in Bristol, Connecticut, where Lucy continued her education at a Catholic school, amid experiences of ethnic divisions in the schooling system.
** relocation, Connecticut, Bristol, education, Catholic school, ethnic divisions **
# Childhood Experiences

> Lucy reflects on her experiences growing up in a predominantly white environment after moving to Connecticut. Despite racial isolation, she shares memorable moments of newfound freedom compared to her restricted childhood in Brooklyn.
** childhood, ethnic isolation, freedom, Connecticut, racial challenges **
# Education Struggles and Achievements

> Discusses the gender and racial challenges Lucy faced in education, touching upon support for her male sibling over her, despite her academic achievements. She shares her experience trying to join extracurricular activities like cheerleading amid racial prejudice.
** education, gender roles, racial prejudice, extracurricular activities, struggles **
# First Marriage and Family Life

> Lucy shares her experience of her first marriage that was partly an escape from family life. She reflects on the cultural and familial context of her decision and life in Hawaii, which led to her first son's birth.
** marriage, family life, Hawaii, cultural context, escape, first son **
# Return and Education Resurgence

> After her first divorce, Lucy returns home and eventually goes back to school at Manchester Community College, supported by scholarships. Her academic journey leads her to a master's degree in sociology.
** divorce, education, Manchester Community College, sociology, scholarships **
# Teaching Career and Community Impact

> Lucy delves into her teaching career at community colleges, emphasizing their role in providing second chances. She recounts her community involvement and contribution to social equality through education.
** teaching, community colleges, education, social equality, impact **
# Religious Journey

> Lucy shares her journey with religion, from her Catholic upbringing to her eventual distancing from organized religion after feeling disillusioned. She discusses finding her spiritual community in the United Methodist Church.
** religion, Catholic, United Methodist Church, spirituality, community **
# Community Involvement in Retirement

> Expressions of gratitude and community involvement are highlighted as Lucy discusses her engagement in gardening, baking, and supporting her neighborhood post-retirement while managing health challenges.
** retirement, community involvement, gardening, baking, health challenges **
# Adapting During COVID
> Lucy discusses how her family adapted during the COVID-19 pandemic, including maintaining a tight-knit family circle, supporting local initiatives, and prioritizing safety and health.
** COVID-19, adaptation, family support, safety, local initiatives **
# Life in Bloomfield
> Lucy describes her life in Bloomfield, moving into the area due to marriage, and experiencing demographic shifts. She shares fond memories and community activities that enriched her experience.
** Bloomfield, community, demographic shifts, fond memories, activities **
# Neighborhood and Community Spirit

> The interview concludes with Lucy reflecting on the strong community spirit and engagement within her neighborhood. She illustrates the shared experiences and close-knit relationships that characterize their lifestyle.
** neighborhood, community spirit, relationships, engagement, lifestyle **

story.title: Lucy Hurston Oral History 03182023.WAV
story.description: In this interview, Lucy Hurston shares her experiences growing up in Brooklyn and later moving to Connecticut, reflecting on the racial dynamics she encountered. She discusses her eclectic career journey, from working in data processing to becoming a sociology professor at community colleges, emphasizing her passion for education and community service. Lucy describes her personal life, including multiple marriages, raising children, and her eventual fulfillment with her current husband. Her commitment to community involvement is highlighted by efforts like starting a community kitchen. She also expresses her views on religion, finding a supportive church community, and her continued dedication to learning and creativity.
